title: Treatment and management of anxiety disorders and fear-related disorders
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Clinical — systematic desensitisation, flooding, CBT, and drugs for anxiety.
learningObjectives: Explain CBT for anxiety and panic.
Assess virtual reality and self-help approaches.

simpleExplanation.title: Facing and fixing fear
simpleExplanation.summary: Behavioural therapies expose patients to feared stimuli gradually; CBT challenges anxious thoughts; drugs reduce physiological arousal.

flashcards.back: 1) Relaxation training. 2) Fear hierarchy (least to most feared). 3) Gradual paired exposure while relaxed — counter-conditioning.
Prolonged, intense exposure to feared stimulus without escape until anxiety **habituates** — faster but more distressing.
Challenge catastrophic misinterpretations of bodily sensations ('racing heart = heart attack'); interoceptive exposure to feared sensations.
Enhance GABA — rapid anxiety reduction; **tolerance and dependence** risk limits long-term use.
First-line pharmacological treatment for GAD, panic, social anxiety — slower onset but lower dependency than benzodiazepines.
Flooding causes high distress and may provoke dropout; SD is **gradual** and more acceptable but **slower**.

sections.content: **Anxiety treatment** (1.4.3) completes 1.4. Compare **SD vs flooding** ethics and effectiveness.

sections.question:
A patient with a spider phobia avoids gardens and has catastrophised thoughts ('It will attack me').

(a) Outline systematic desensitisation and CBT for this patient. [4 marks]
(b) Compare systematic desensitisation with flooding. Evaluate the use of benzodiazepines for anxiety. [6 marks]
sections.solution:
(a) **AO1 — Systematic desensitisation:**
- Train **relaxation** (e.g. breathing).
- Build **hierarchy**: spider picture → video → jar at distance → closer exposure.
- Pair each step with relaxation — **counter-conditioning** replaces fear with calm.

**AO1 — CBT:**
- Identify **catastrophic thoughts** ('It will attack me').
- **Evidence testing** — challenge probability and coping ability.
- **Behavioural experiments** during graded exposure.

(b) **AO3 — SD vs flooding:**
| | SD | Flooding |
|---|---|---|
| **Process** | Gradual hierarchy | Immediate full exposure |
| **Distress** | Low–moderate | Very high initially |
| **Ethics** | More acceptable | Risk of dropout, retraumatisation |
| **Effectiveness** | Strong evidence | Can work if patient persists |

**AO3 — Benzodiazepines evaluation:**
- **Strengths:** **Rapid** relief of acute anxiety; useful short-term for crisis.
- **Limitations:** **Tolerance**, **dependence**, withdrawal rebound anxiety; do not address **maintaining** avoidance cognitively.
- **Comparison:** SSRIs preferred long-term; **CBT/SD** produce durable effects without dependency.

**Judgement:** SD + CBT is **first-line** for this phobia; benzodiazepines at most **short-term adjunct**, not primary treatment.
